title: Println vs Printf — What's the Difference?
instruction:
In Chapter 1 you used `fmt.Println()`. Now meet its sibling: `fmt.Printf()`.

- `Println` adds a newline automatically and uses default formatting.
- `Printf` gives you full control — you write a template with `%s`, `%d`, `%f` placeholders.

The variables `name` and `age` are already set (you saw these in Chapter 2!). Use `fmt.Printf` to print `"Name: Alice, Age: 17"` on one line.
hint: fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Age: %d\n", name, age) — %s for string, %d for integer, \n for newline.

title: Format a Float — Control Decimal Places
instruction:
Alice's GPA is `3.8`, but the school system displays it as `3.80` — always two decimal places. Use `%.2f` to control how many decimal places are shown.

Print `"GPA: 3.80"` using Printf.
hint: fmt.Printf("GPA: %.2f\n", gpa) — the .2 before f means exactly 2 decimal places.

title: Print a Full Student Profile
instruction:
Combine everything from Chapter 2. Use Printf to print a formatted student profile — one field per line:
```
Name:     Alice
Age:      17
GPA:      3.80
Enrolled: true
```
hint: Call fmt.Printf four times, one for each field. Use %s, %d, %.2f, and %t (for booleans).
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name := "Alice"
	age := 17
	gpa := 3.8
	enrolled := true
	// TODO: use Printf four times to print the profile above
	_ = name
	_ = age
	_ = gpa
	_ = enrolled
}

title: Build a String with Sprintf
instruction:
Sometimes you want to **build** a string and store it — not print it immediately. `fmt.Sprintf()` works exactly like Printf but returns the result as a string.

Build Alice's ID badge: `"[1042] Alice — Grade 11"` using Sprintf with the variables below, then print it.
hint: badge := fmt.Sprintf("[%d] %s — Grade %d", id, name, grade) — then fmt.Println(badge).
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	id := 1042
	name := "Alice"
	grade := 11
	// TODO: use Sprintf to build "[1042] Alice — Grade 11" and store in badge
	badge := ""
	_ = id
	_ = name
	_ = grade
	fmt.Println(badge)
}

title: Print a Separator Line
instruction: The school report needs a clean separator between sections. Use `fmt.Printf` with `%s` and the `strings.Repeat` function to print a line of 30 dashes: `"------------------------------"`.
hint: fmt.Printf("%s\n", strings.Repeat("-", 30)) — strings.Repeat repeats a string N times.

title: Print the Type of a Variable
instruction:
The verb `%T` prints the Go **type** of any value — super useful when debugging. Print Alice's score (`83`) and its type on one line:
```
value: 83, type: int
```
hint: fmt.Printf("value: %v, type: %T\n", score, score) — %v is the default format, %T is the type.

title: Build the Full Report
instruction:
Put it all together. Use Sprintf to build a one-line summary string for Alice's full report:
```
Report | Alice | Score: 83 | GPA: 3.80
```
Store it in `report` and print it.
hint: fmt.Sprintf("Report | %s | Score: %d | GPA: %.2f", name, score, gpa)
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name := "Alice"
	score := 83
	gpa := 3.8
	// TODO: use Sprintf to build the report string
	report := ""
	_ = name
	_ = score
	_ = gpa
	fmt.Println(report)
}
